q: What is a type annotation in TypeScript?
a: A type annotation is a label you add after a colon that tells TypeScript what kind of value a variable holds, like a number or a string. If you later try to put the wrong kind of value in, TypeScript warns you before the code runs.
fs: An annotation (name: type) is a compile-time promise; the checker flags any assignment that breaks it, with zero runtime cost.
fj: Thinking the annotation changes the value or exists at runtime. It is erased when compiled to JavaScript; it only guides the checker.

q: What is type inference in TypeScript?
a: Type inference is TypeScript guessing the type for you so you don't have to write it. If you assign a value when you declare a variable, TypeScript reads that value and remembers its type automatically.
fs: Inference derives a type from the initial value, so explicit annotations are mostly needed only where context is missing (e.g. params).
fj: Assuming you must annotate everything. Over-annotating obvious values is noise; let inference handle initialized variables.

q: What is an interface in TypeScript?
a: An interface is a named shape that describes what properties an object must have and what type each one is. Once defined, you can reuse it to type many objects and TypeScript checks each one matches.
fs: An interface names the shape of an object; the checker enforces every required key and its type wherever the interface is used.
fj: Confusing an interface with a class. It holds no data or logic and creates nothing at runtime; it only describes a shape.

q: What is a union type in TypeScript?
a: A union type lets a value be one of several types, written with a vertical bar between them. For example string | number means the value may be a string or a number, and nothing else.
fs: A union (A | B) widens a value to several allowed types; you must narrow it (e.g. typeof checks) before using type-specific members.
fj: Treating a union value as if it were already one type. Until you narrow it, only members common to all options are allowed.

code.label: a value that can be two types

q: What is an optional property in TypeScript?
a: An optional property is a field that may or may not be present on an object, marked with a question mark after its name. Code that uses it must handle the case where it is missing (undefined).
fs: name?: T means T | undefined and the key may be absent; the checker forces you to guard before treating it as present.
fj: Assuming an optional property always has a value. It can be undefined, so accessing it directly without a check can break.
